name: Deterministic LL/LR
summary: Classical grammar-driven deterministic parsing families: top-down LL (predictive, leftmost derivation) and bottom-up LR (shift-reduce, rightmost derivation in reverse). Still the vocabulary of compiler front-ends even when the production parser is hand-written.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
The classic parsing families are still the vocabulary of compiler front-ends even when a production parser is hand-written.

LL parsers read left-to-right and construct a leftmost derivation; they are naturally top-down and align with recursive descent. `LL(1)` uses one token of lookahead, `LL(k)` uses fixed k-token lookahead, and `LL(*)` / `ALL(*)` generalise the lookahead story.

LR parsers read left-to-right and construct a rightmost derivation in reverse; they are bottom-up and operate as shift/reduce automata. The family includes `LR(0)`, `SLR(1)`, `LALR(1)`, canonical `LR(1)`, `LR(k)`, and practical variants such as Bison's `IELR(1)`. `SLR` is compact but weak, `LALR(1)` is the yacc-era sweet spot, canonical `LR(1)` has better conflict precision but larger tables, and `IELR(1)` aims to preserve canonical LR(1) language recognition with LALR-like table size.

sections.title: LL vs LR intuition
sections.content:
LL approaches are top-down and predictive — at each non-terminal, lookahead picks the production. Grammar engineering for LL means eliminating left recursion and left-factoring common prefixes so that a lookahead token selects one production.

LR approaches are bottom-up: a shift/reduce automaton accumulates terminals and recognises right-hand sides as they complete. LR generators surface conflicts statically as shift/reduce or reduce/reduce reports, which is the chief diagnostic advantage of the family.

sections.title: Where ambiguity is discovered
sections.content:
For language design, the key question is not "which acronym is best?" but "where should ambiguity be discovered?"

- LL-style parsers tend to put ambiguity resolution in source-code control flow (the parser author chooses).
- LR-style generators surface conflicts statically as shift/reduce or reduce/reduce reports.
- Generalized parsers (GLR, GLL, Earley) accept ambiguity and return a forest.
- PEG bakes priority into ordered choice.

A language designer can intentionally target an LL/RD-friendly grammar for implementation simplicity, an LR-friendly grammar for conflict diagnostics and generator support, or a more expressive syntax with a generalized or PEG parser.

sections.title: Relevance to tooling
sections.content: Classical deterministic parsing remains foundational, but interactive tooling usually adds stronger recovery, incremental updates, and partial-parse tolerance on top. Editor-resilient parsers (tree-sitter, Lezer, rust-analyzer's resilient LL) extend the LR/LL substrate with parallel error recovery, incremental tree reuse, and ERROR-node retention.
